scholarly.angle: Era 19 — Maurya Empire & Ashoka (322–185 BCE): Ashoka's rock and pillar edicts (c. 268–232 BCE) represent a distinctive category of ancient imperial inscription — neither legal codes (Hammurabi), nor ritual-cosmic proclamations (Zhou Mandate-of-Heaven), nor royal-achievement inscriptions (Achaemenid Behistun, Assyrian annals) — but an explicit programme of state-promulgated ethical instruction (dhamma) directed at both administrators and general subjects, invoking welfare as the sovereign's self-defined duty.
scholarly.key_claim: Ashoka's rock and pillar edicts (c. 268–232 BCE) constitute a sui generis category of state-promulgated ethical instruction (dhamma), distinct in function from Hammurabi-style legal codes, Zhou ritual-cosmic rhetoric, and Achaemenid / Assyrian royal-achievement inscriptions; the corpus addresses both administrative cadres and general subjects and invokes the sovereign's self-defined duty of welfare (parakrama).

scholarly_debate.question: What is the appropriate category for Ashoka's rock and pillar edicts, and do they represent genuine ethical governance reaching subjects, elite-administrator signalling, or a Buddhist conversion programme?
scholarly_debate.position_a: Edicts are a distinctive category of state-promulgated ethical instruction (dhamma) invoking a Buddhist-inflected but inclusivist ethic of universal welfare, reaching administrators and subjects alike (Thapar 1961/2012; Lahiri 2015; Bhargava 2023 — 'expansive moral hegemony'; Panduranga Bhatta 2000).
scholarly_debate.position_b: Edicts are primarily a biopolitical / governance tool — philologically sophisticated moral propaganda directed at sub-imperial administrators and regional elites, with 'Ashoka-as-Buddhist-universalist' being a modern projection; the Maurya polity itself was an overlord-tributary network, not a uniform administration (Squarcini 2019; Sugandhi 2012; Smith, Gillespie & Barron 2016 — locational geography of edicts).
scholarly_debate.current_consensus: Edicts are a distinctive inscriptional category — neither legal code nor royal-achievement inscription — but scholarly consensus accepts hybrid intent: ethical programme plus political-administrative tool. Their derivation from an Achaemenid epigraphic precedent (Sahu 2018) is conceded while content/audience is agreed to be essentially Indian / Mauryan in character.
scholarly_debate.resolution_needed: Fine-grained epigraphic audience analysis — especially philologically rigorous treatment of Prakrit diffusion (Norman), the Minor vs Major Rock Edict distinction (Gaál & Tóth 2018), and locational archaeology of inscription siting (Smith, Gillespie & Barron 2016; Falk 2006) — to triangulate who actually could and did receive the messages.

contested_claims.claim: Ashoka was a genuine Buddhist convert after Kalinga vs. a pragmatic inclusivist ruler whose dhamma is distinct from sectarian Buddhism.
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Kumar (2022) reads RE XIII and Mahavamsa harmonistically as confirming conversion c. 265 BCE; Squarcini (2019) and Bhargava (2023) treat 'Ashoka-as-Buddhist-universalist' as a politically serviceable reconstruction (modern reception debt to Sen); Lahiri (2015, reviewed McClish 2017) complicates the hagiography; no edict quotes canonical Buddhist sutras verbatim.

contested_claims.claim: Edicts functioned as mass governance (participatory ethical monarchy) vs. elite-administrator signalling.
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Smith, Gillespie & Barron (2016) locational geography finds edicts deliberately placed along routeways, coasts and mineral zones — consistent with reaching administrators and trade-network travellers; Panduranga Bhatta (2000) reads them as leadership-values addressed to all subjects; Gaál & Tóth (2018) show Minor Rock Edicts absorbed formulaic language from Major Rock Edicts — evidence of scribal reproduction across audience-types.

contested_claims.claim: Arthashastra is a Kautilya / Chanakya-era composition (c. 4th c. BCE) vs. a multi-layered compilation reaching final form c. 1st–3rd c. CE (Olivelle 2013).
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Haldipur (2018) and Venkatachalam, Nielsen & Modi (2022) explicitly date the text 'sometime between 300 and 100 BC (Olivelle 2013; Doniger 2014)' and '2nd century BCE', reflecting post-2013 acceptance that the text is composite; Sihag (2007) and Škare (2013) continue the older 4th-c-BCE Kautilya-authorship position.

contested_claims.claim: Maurya imperial reach — uniform centralised administration (Arthashastra as operating manual) vs. overlord-tributary network (Fussman-style critique).
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Sugandhi (2012) explicitly reframes Mauryan imperialism as 'a relational network perspective … less to do with territorial control'; Sharma (2018) on Deccan archaeology reassesses whether on-ground data match uniform-administration claims; Rooney & Murthy (2020) treat the Arthashastra-prescribed institutions as ideal-types, not necessarily implemented.

contested_claims.claim: Maurya decline: structural (imperial over-extension / fiscal exhaustion) vs. ideological (Buddhist turn alienating Brahmin elites) vs. opportunistic (Pushyamitra Shunga's coup).
contested_claims.evidentiary_basis: Retrieved literature acknowledges all three causal frames without adjudicating; Thapar's structural thesis is the backbone anchor cited (indirectly) across multiple retrieved papers (e.g. Smith et al. 2016; McDonagh et al. 2020); no single paper in this corpus systematically adjudicates decline causes.

evidence.title: Finding history: the locational geography of Ashokan inscriptions in the Indian subcontinent
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: journal-article in recognised field venue (Antiquity) but citingPublicationsCount<30; no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: GIS-based locational analysis of Ashokan edict sites shows deliberate siting along routes, coasts, and mineral-rich zones (Kalinga, south-central peninsula, Kolar gold fields); supports the 'strategic audience' reading while explicitly foregrounding Thapar's 1961/1997 anchor text and Falk (2006).
evidence.excerpt: In South Asia, the first stone inscriptions occur in the third century BC, coincident with the appearance of the Mauryan polity in the northern Gangetic plain (Allchin & Norman 1985; Thapar 1997 [1961], 2004 [2002], 2006; Falk 2006). The inscriptions are known as Major and Minor Rock Edicts and consist of repetitive copies of texts attributed to the Mauryan leader Ashoka.

evidence.title: Some "Major" trends in Aśoka's Minor Rock Edicts
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: journal-article in recognised philological venue; no editorial notices; central to Kandahar bilingual debate (Era 18 bridge)
evidence.key_finding: Philological argument that the Greco-Aramaic Kandahar edict should be re-categorised as a Minor Rock Edict (not Major) on formal, locational, and textual grounds — directly supports the claim that the edict corpus has layered audiences (administrators, scribes, Greek-speaking populations at westernmost frontier). Key Era 18 / Hellenistic bridge paper.
evidence.excerpt: Greco-Aramaic Edict from Kandahar… a bilingual text in Greek and Aramaic, we are in much greater trouble with ascertaining its nature… a number of characteristics would qualify this edict for being a remote counterpart, adapted to Greek and Aramaic languages, of MRE I… the location of the edict in old Kandahar, at the westernmost border of Aśoka's empire, seems perfectly to fit the requirement expressed in MRE I that the edict should be engraved with a view that even the farthest 'ends' might get to know the message.

evidence.title: A New Interpretation of the Edicts of Aśoka from Kandahar
evidence.journal: Annali di Ca' Foscari, Serie Orientale
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: specialist journal but low citingPublicationsCount; no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Proposes a new completion for the opening lacuna in the Greek section of the Kandahar bilingual and questions whether the Greek texts are translations of an original or independently composed — directly relevant to Hellenistic-koine bridge to Era 18. Recounts the other Aramaic edicts of the Afghan territory (Pol-i Darūnta, Laghmān).
evidence.excerpt: From Alexandria of Arachosia, present-day Kandahar, we have two edicts in Greek, issued by the sovereign Maurya Aśoka (c. 270-230 BC)… The first edict -discovered in 1957 -is in two languages, Greek and Aramaic, while the second -discovered in 1963 -is in Greek alone.

evidence.title: Conquests of Dharma: Network Models and the Study of Ancient Polities
evidence.journal: Archeological Papers of the American Anthropological Association
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: anthropological-archaeology venue, citingPublicationsCount<30 but cited across corpus; no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Systematic reframing of the Mauryan empire as a relational network rather than a uniformly administered territorial polity — the core Fussman-aligned 'overlord-tributary' critique in a citable, post-2010 anthropological idiom. Directly underwrites contested claim #4.
evidence.excerpt: The Mauryan Empire, an early South Asian polity, was once presumed to have exerted control over most of the Indian subcontinent. A reexamination of both archaeological and historical evidence suggests a different interpretation of Mauryan imperialism — one that has less to do with territorial control and instead looks to a relational network perspective.

evidence.title: Selling tolerance by the pound: On ideal types' fragility, Aśoka's edicts and the political theology of toleration in and beyond South Asia
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: recognised philosophy/political-theory venue; no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Close reading of Rock Edict XIII and a corrective to Sen-influenced portraits of Ashoka as universalist liberal: reframes the edicts as 'governance utility played by blaming and praising biopolitical devices' — a sophisticated statement of the 'edicts as elite-administrator governance tool' position.
evidence.excerpt: The close reading of Aśoka's edicts definitely testify the mastery on political matters of its author, which had a rather lucid understanding of the governance utility played by blaming and praising biopolitical devices.

evidence.title: Ashoka's Dhamma as a Project of Expansive Moral Hegemony
evidence.journal: Bridging Two Worlds (Luminos / University of California Press)
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: peer-reviewed OA academic press volume with established editors; no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Frames dhamma as an 'expansive moral hegemony' project — a precise contemporary articulation of the 'state-promulgated ethical instruction' category claim this agent was asked to evaluate. CC-BY-ND open-access chapter.

evidence.title: Aśokan edicts: The genesis of the imperial idea and culture in early India and the debt to Iran
evidence.key_finding: Concedes the Achaemenid epigraphic precedent (Darius/Xerxes stone inscriptions) but argues the contexts and contents of Ashokan inscriptions are essentially different from Achaemenid royal-achievement annals — directly supports the sharpened key claim's contrast to Behistun-style inscriptions.
evidence.excerpt: This essay concedes the connection but argues that the contexts and contents of Aśokan inscriptions were essentially different from their Achaemenid precedents, and Mauryan art too, in both its forms and message, owed much to indigenous tastes and genius.

evidence.title: Book Review: Nayanjot Lahiri, Ashoka in Ancient India
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: book review, low citation density but confirms presence of Lahiri's Tier-1 monograph in the scholarly record
evidence.key_finding: Scholarly review confirming the publication and reception of Lahiri's Ashoka in Ancient India (Permanent Black, 2015) — an expected-literature anchor that Scite does not index as a monograph but that is surfaced via reviews.

evidence.title: Book Review: Searching for Ashoka: Questing for a Buddhist King from India to Thailand (Lahiri 2023)
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: book review; confirms Lahiri's 2023 follow-up monograph linking Ashoka to Southeast Asian Buddhist transmission (Stargardt-adjacent concern)
evidence.key_finding: Documents Lahiri's 2023 SUNY Press monograph extending the Ashoka question into Southeast Asia — the closest Scite-indexed approach to the Stargardt (Southeast Asian Buddhist transmission) thread in the expected-literature list.

evidence.title: Asokan inscriptions: A bird-eye view
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: minor venue (AkiNik); low indexed citation footprint
evidence.key_finding: Restates the conventional Kalinga-conversion narrative using Rock Edict XIII and the Mahavamsa; useful as a statement of position A on Ashoka's religious identity — not a leading scholarly voice but shows the narrative's continuing currency.
evidence.excerpt: On the basis of these inscriptions, we come to the conclusion that after Asoka's conquest of Kalinga, the desire and dedication for Buddhism became very intense. Asoka was a follower of Buddhism even before the conquest of Kalinga. But till then Ashoka did not give much importance to religion.

evidence.title: Addiction: a brief history of an idea
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities (cross-disciplinary use): document surfacing the Olivelle 2013 Arthashastra dating; not a humanities-specialist paper but cites the key authorship datum
evidence.key_finding: Explicitly cites Olivelle (2013) and Doniger (2014) for the Arthashastra's dating to 'sometime between 300 and 100 BC' — a concise corroboration that the post-Olivelle composite-dating view is now embedded in cross-disciplinary literature.
evidence.excerpt: The Arthashastra (Kautilya, 2016), written sometime between 300 and 100 BC (Olivelle 2013; Doniger, 2014). Addiction was considered to be a hindrance to good governance — a notion redolent of the present-day interest among lawmakers the world over to enact laws governing the manufacture, sale, and use of various psychoactive substances.

evidence.title: The politics of gifts and reciprocity in South–South Cooperation: The case of India's Covid-19 diplomacy
evidence.tier_rule_applied: social (cross-disciplinary): journal not a core humanities venue but surfaces Arthashastra authorship / dating debate
evidence.key_finding: Dates the Arthashastra as 'a second century bce text authored by Kautilya' — illustrates the persistence of the older Kautilya-authorship position alongside Olivelle's composite view, evidence that the authorship debate remains live in 2020s political-theory usage.
evidence.excerpt: There has been a particular resurgence of interest in the Arthashastra, a second century bce text authored by Kautilya (350–275 bce), the prime minister of Chandragupta Maurya I in the Maurya period (r. c.321–c.297 bce).

evidence.title: Institutions, social order and wealth in ancient India
evidence.tier_rule_applied: social (applied institutions): CUP journal, low citation density
evidence.key_finding: Treats the Mauryan polity via Arthashastra and Greek/Roman sources as an institutional case; useful cross-check for contested claim #4 (Maurya imperial reach) — acknowledges the Arthashastra's ideal-type character without committing to its empirical implementation.

evidence.title: Mauryan Intervention in the Deccan: A Study of Archaeological Data
evidence.journal: Journal of Advances and Scholarly Researches in Allied Education
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: minor venue, but topically relevant to Deccan-reach debate
evidence.key_finding: Re-examines archaeological data from the Deccan against the 'uniform empire' hypothesis; evidence that the secondary-state-formation / relational-network interpretation is gaining regional purchase.
evidence.excerpt: In the current historiography, it has been argued that it encouraged secondary state formation in the new regions like Deccan and Kalinga (Orissa).

evidence.title: Leadership Values: Insights from Ashoka's Inscriptions
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: citingPublicationsCount>10; SAGE journal; no editorial notices
evidence.key_finding: Treats the edicts as a substantive ethical-leadership programme ('effective leadership depends not on preaching moral values but on practising them') — represents the 'edicts as mass governance / ethical instruction' position and has been widely cited (14 mentioning citations).

evidence.title: Kautilya on Administration of Justice During the Fourth Century B.C.
evidence.journal: Journal of the History of Economic Thought
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities/social: CUP journal; explicit mentioning-citation discussing 'date and authorship of the Arthashastra' — relevant to contested claim #3
evidence.key_finding: Defends the pre-Olivelle Kautilya authorship position and treats the Arthashastra as a coherent 4th-century BCE composition; useful counterweight to Olivelle 2013 in contested-claim #3 evidence base.

evidence.title: On the Significance of Religion for Global Diplomacy
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: academic press monograph; low citation density but explicit citation to Thapar 1961 and treatment of Rock & Pillar Edicts
evidence.key_finding: Cites Thapar (1961) and summarises Ashoka as 'promoting peace through dharma, the inner law that shapes all being, and is, at the same time, a humane code of conduct embracing all aspects of life' — a mid-2020 restatement of the Thapar-anchored ethical-governance interpretation.
evidence.excerpt: In the 3rd century BC, in a development of the Buddhist tradition, the Emperor Ashoka promotes peace through dharma, the inner law that shapes all being, and is, at the same time, a humane code of conduct embracing all aspects of life (Thapar, 1961). Ashoka commissions a series of inscriptions, the so-called Rock Edicts and Pillar Edicts, to make known the elements of dharma throughout his domains.

evidence.title: Az epigráfiai hagyomány kezdetei Indiában, különös tekintettel a görög–indiai kulturális érintkezésekre a hellenisztikus kor évszázadaiban [The beginnings of epigraphic tradition in India, with special attention to Greek–Indian cultural contact in the Hellenistic centuries]
evidence.tier_rule_applied: humanities: doctoral dissertation; authoritative philological synthesis but limited Scite-indexed citations
evidence.key_finding: ELTE doctoral dissertation synthesising the Indian epigraphic tradition with explicit focus on Greek–Indian Hellenistic contact — comprehensive review of the Kandahar bilingual / Minor Rock Edict debate (Salomon, Falk, Schmitt, Thapar). Direct bridge to Era 18 koine.
evidence.excerpt: Ráadásul láthattuk, hogy Kandahār óvárosában a nagy sziklaediktumokat kőlapra írták fel, nem sziklára, ugyanolyan módon, ahogy a nagy sziklaediktumok összes többi helyszínén, azaz több darabból álló sorozatban.

gaps_observed: Search 1 (citing_publications_from=30) returned mostly off-topic cross-discipline noise (ESG, social entrepreneurship citing the Ashoka Foundation, not the emperor). The high citation-floor filter mis-surfaced modern governance literature. Adaptive note: for humanities era queries, omit the citing_publications_from floor.

Thapar's Aśoka and the Decline of the Mauryas (1961/2012), the Tier-1 anchor, is not directly indexed by Scite as a standalone record; it is present only as citations-within-citations (Smith et al. 2016; McDonagh et al. 2020; Squarcini 2019). Monograph not retrievable via DOI in this corpus.

Olivelle (2013), King, Governance, and Law in Ancient India, similarly does not surface as a standalone Scite record; only indirectly via Haldipur (2018) and Venkatachalam et al. (2022) dating citations.

No retrieved paper in this corpus systematically adjudicates the three Maurya-decline theses (structural / ideological / opportunistic). This is a literature-thinness finding for the decline question.

Stargardt (Southeast Asian Buddhist transmission) did not surface directly; the nearest proxy is Lahiri's 2023 SUNY Press monograph (reviewed in Falarti 2025) and Neelis's Early Buddhist Transmission and Trade Networks (2011, Brill, DOI 10.1163/ej.9789004181595.i-372).
Norman's Prakrit dialectology is referenced within Tóth 2018 and Gaál & Tóth 2018 but does not appear as a separately retrievable record in this search round.
Falk's Aśokan Sites and Artefacts (2006) appears only as an in-text citation across multiple retrieved papers — again not directly indexed at monograph level.
Fussman's critical 'loose overlordship' position is best represented by Sugandhi (2012) as a proxy; Fussman's own papers do not surface in this search round.

Sharma, A. (2018). Mauryan intervention in the Deccan: A study of archaeological data. *Journal of Advances and Scholarly Researches in Allied Education*, *14*(2), 1–6. https://doi.org/10.29070/jasrae.v14.i2.2018.1-6
